title: RFC 9309 Robots Semantics and Library Conformance Adapter · Compiler Case: Extension Restraint
summary: The crawler fetches robots from the wrong origin, trusts a library without conformance fixtures, applies first-match rules, and treats outages as permission. Predict authorization, URL identity, runtime admission, Promise ownership, resource bounds, accessibility impact, failure, and evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the crawler ts extension restraint increment in RFC 9309 decision adapter around robots-parser. Learners progress through prediction, e…
objectives: Treat crawl-delay, request-rate, sitemap, and other extensions as documented policy inputs without claiming RFC 9309 requires them.
Wrap robots-parser behind owned types and test its outputs against RFC 9309 fixtures before accepting library decisions.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Treat crawl-delay, request-rate, sitemap, and other extensions as documented policy inputs without claiming RFC 9309 requires them.

hints: For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Extension Restraint model · Theory Extension Res…, name the owner, authorization, source and emitted revision, Node, TypeScript, URL, robots, unknown value, Fetch, Promise, AbortSignal, browser, accessibility, report, handle, recovery, and native-transfer boundary, Extension Restraint precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Extension Restraint model · Theory Extension Res…, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A theory TypeScript team handles case 5549 during a multilingual archive markup migration. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove RFC 9309 product, group, encoding, longest-match, response-state, cache, and library adapter behavior; every external value remains unknown until runtime admission produces an owned value; then change one owner permission, seed, URL, robots, sitemap, redirect, DNS, media, or encoding decision and reconcile provenance. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Treat crawl-delay, request-rate, sitemap, and other extensions as documented policy inputs without claiming RFC 9309 requires them.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Extension Restraint model · Theory Extension Res…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: crawl-delay is a normative RFC 9309 directive with universal behavior.

checks.description: The cumulative typescript portfolio contains this competency’s named evidence increment.
checks.failureMessage: Add the exact evidence marker // Evidence: crawler-ts-extension-restraint-aaovgj.

checks.pattern: // Evidence: crawler-ts-extension-restraint-aaovgj(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?function\s+evidence_crawler_ts_extension_restraint_aaovgj\s*\([^)]*\)\s*:\s*Readonly<\{[^}]+\}>\s*\{(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?robotsFixtures < 15)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?conformantFixtures < 15 \|\| conformantFixtures > robotsFixtures)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?failureCases < 6)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?unsafeAllows > 0)(?=(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?return)(?:(?!// Evidence:)[\s\S])*?return
checks.description: The increment includes the required executable or reviewable evidence structure.
